_meta.purpose: Long-form rubric for matching a job description to a 1-5 level per NACE competency via embedding + cosine similarity. Embed each level 'description' and compare to the JD (or the JD's competency-relevant text). Highest cosine similarity wins; its level number is the score.
_meta.version: long (expanded descriptions for sharper embedding separation)
_meta.scale: 1 = basic/incidental demand; 5 = competency is central, high-stakes, and the role could not be done without it
_meta.embedding_note: Embed the 'description' fields only. The 'definition' field is for humans and should NOT be embedded, to avoid polluting the level vectors with shared competency vocabulary.
_meta.design_note: Each level is expanded with concrete role types, activities, audiences, stakes, and the kinds of phrasing real job descriptions use at that intensity. The added text is distinguishing detail (different audiences, tasks, consequences), not repeated intensity adjectives, so adjacent levels stay separable. Level 1 describes the KIND of low-demand role rather than asserting 'not required', so real JDs that simply omit the competency still match against it.
_meta.known_limitation_negation: Pure cosine similarity keys off topic, not negation. A JD saying a skill is explicitly NOT required can still match a mid/high level. Use the optional LLM check to catch negation and relative emphasis.
_meta.known_limitation_calibration: Per-competency max similarity is not comparable across competencies (one may top out at 0.30, another at 0.80). Normalize per-competency before combining into any weighted average or cross-competency ranking.

career_self_development.definition: Demand the role places on owning one's learning and career growth: seeking feedback, building skills, networking, and developing professionally.

levels.1: Growth and self-development are incidental. The postholder is asked mainly to learn the immediate tasks and then perform them as taught, with little structured development beyond initial onboarding and no expectation to chart a path forward. Feedback is occasional and informal, and any improvement tends to come from repetition rather than deliberate effort. The role does not ask the person to seek out learning opportunities, build a professional network, or think about where their skills are heading. Steady performance of a known job is what matters, and career-mindedness is neither required nor especially rewarded. This suits roles where the work is stable and the person is expected simply to do it well.
levels.2: Some willingness to learn and improve is expected. The postholder takes on occasional training, acts on basic feedback when it is given, and picks up new tools or methods as the job requires, but development is largely reactive and prompted by a manager rather than self-driven. The role values being teachable and adaptable enough to keep pace with everyday change, without asking the person to set their own learning goals or actively manage their growth. Building skills happens in service of current duties rather than a longer trajectory. There is little expectation of networking, self-promotion, or deliberate career planning. The job rewards someone who improves steadily when guided.
levels.3: Taking ownership of one's own development is a real and expected part of the role. The postholder is expected to seek feedback, build relevant skills, keep up with changes in the field, and take some responsibility for their own progress rather than waiting to be told what to learn. They are trusted to identify gaps, pursue training or resources, and adapt as the work evolves. Some self-direction in learning genuinely helps, and the role assumes the person will keep growing into broader or deeper responsibilities over time. Engaging with peers, mentors, or professional communities may be encouraged. A self-improving, growth-minded attitude is part of doing the job well.
levels.4: Active self-development and career ownership are important to the role. The postholder is expected to drive their own learning, pursue relevant qualifications or certifications, build and use professional networks, and stay deliberately current in a moving field. They take charge of where their skills need to go next, turn feedback into concrete improvement, and seek out stretch opportunities rather than waiting for them. The role rewards initiative in professional growth and assumes the person will invest in their own development as a matter of habit. Visibility, reputation, and relationships within and beyond the organization start to matter. Someone passive about their own growth would struggle to keep up.
levels.5: Continuous, self-driven growth is central to the role and largely defines success in it. The postholder must learn rapidly and constantly, actively manage their own career and development, build strong professional networks, and treat upskilling as a core ongoing responsibility rather than an occasional activity. The role often sits in a fast-moving environment where staying expert requires sustained, deliberate effort, and where the person's trajectory, visibility, and relationships directly shape the opportunities open to them and the organization. They may be expected to set their own direction, seek out mentorship and sponsorship, and represent themselves credibly to advance the work. The kind of role described as a fast-track, high-growth, or self-directed development track fits here. Without a strong drive to learn, network, and grow, the role could not be sustained.

communication.definition: Demand the role places on clear writing, speaking, listening, presenting, and tailoring messages to different audiences.

levels.1: Communication is incidental and low-effort. The postholder exchanges brief, simple information with an immediate supervisor or a small, familiar team, mostly to confirm tasks, log progress, or ask straightforward questions. Written work is limited to short emails, checklists, or status notes, and spoken interaction rarely extends beyond people they already know. The role is about following instructions rather than explaining, persuading, or presenting, and there is no contact with clients, partners, or external audiences. Being clear and pleasant to deal with helps, but the job does not stand or fall on communication skill. This fits hands-on or task-focused roles where the work largely speaks for itself.
levels.2: Routine communication within a team is a normal part of the day. The postholder coordinates regularly with colleagues, writes clear everyday emails and short reports, documents what they do, and takes part in team meetings, stand-ups, or handovers. Audiences are internal and familiar, and the content is operational rather than persuasive. The role rewards being organized, responsive, and easy to work with, but communication supports the work rather than being its main feature. There is little need to tailor a message for unfamiliar readers, present formally, or handle difficult conversations. Solid everyday clarity is enough.
levels.3: Communication is a regular and important part of the role, running through much of the work. The postholder writes documents for varied readers, such as summaries, guidance, or specifications, presents updates to internal groups, and explains their work clearly to people who are not specialists in it. They coordinate across more than one team, facilitate discussions, and keep different parties aligned, so clarity, structure, and responsiveness genuinely affect outcomes. Gaps in communication would slow the work or cause confusion. The role suits someone comfortable translating between audiences and keeping information moving.
levels.4: Communication is central to the role and largely outward-facing. The postholder presents frequently, writes persuasively in proposals, pitches, or reports, and deals directly with clients, partners, or senior stakeholders. They are expected to read the room, adapt the same message for very different audiences, field questions in real time, and represent their team or function to people outside it. Influence, relationship management, and lower-stakes negotiation are part of the job, and how things are communicated materially shapes results. This is the kind of role described as client-facing, stakeholder-facing, or communications-heavy.
levels.5: Communication is the core of the role and carries high stakes. The postholder regularly addresses large audiences, leads important client or partner relationships, negotiates significant agreements, manages conflict, and influences senior decision-makers, executives, or the public. Exceptional written and verbal fluency is essential, often across cultures, in sensitive situations, and under real time or reputational pressure. They may act as a spokesperson, lead difficult negotiations, or drive alignment among powerful and competing parties. The success of the work depends directly on the quality and credibility of their communication, and the role would be impossible for a weak communicator.

critical_thinking.definition: Demand the role places on analysis, evaluation, reasoning, judgment, and using information and data to make sound decisions.

levels.1: Minimal reasoning is needed. The work involves straightforward choices among a few obvious options, basic checking, and applying simple rules, with no real evaluation of evidence or weighing of trade-offs. The postholder mostly recognizes familiar situations and responds in the standard way, and harder calls are passed upward. Where judgment is required it is light and bounded. The role rewards attention, accuracy, and consistency more than analysis. It fits work that is well-defined and procedural.
levels.2: Some analytical thinking is part of everyday work. The postholder interprets routine information, applies set criteria to make ordinary decisions, and spots obvious problems within a familiar scope. They are trusted to make sensible day-to-day calls without checking everything, but the situations are mostly well understood and the right answer is usually findable with modest effort. Deeper evaluation, ambiguity, and high-consequence judgment are not central. The role values being methodical and level-headed within known boundaries.
levels.3: Critical thinking is a regular and important demand of the role. The postholder analyzes information, weighs options, diagnoses problems that are not obvious, and makes reasoned decisions that affect their own work and that of their team. They are expected to investigate causes, consider alternatives, and justify their choices rather than follow a script, and may use data to support what they recommend. The work involves genuine judgment under everyday uncertainty, where a poor decision has real but recoverable consequences. Sound reasoning is part of doing the job well.
levels.4: Rigorous analysis and judgment are central to the role. The postholder evaluates complex or ambiguous information, assesses risks and trade-offs, builds well-supported conclusions and recommendations, and makes decisions with significant consequences. They are expected to handle situations with no clear right answer, reconcile competing considerations, and defend their reasoning to others. The work often involves analysis, strategy, diagnosis, or data-driven decision-making where the quality of thinking directly determines results. This is the kind of role described as analytical, strategic, or requiring strong problem-solving.
levels.5: Advanced reasoning is the core of the role and the basis on which it is judged. The postholder tackles highly complex, ambiguous, or high-stakes problems, synthesizes conflicting evidence, builds strategy, and makes critical judgments that others depend on. They operate where the questions are hard, the information is incomplete or contradictory, and the cost of error is serious. The role might involve setting strategy, leading complex analysis or research, or making decisions that shape direction at a senior level. Deep analytical rigor and sound judgment under uncertainty are essential, and weak reasoning would make the role untenable.

equity_inclusion.definition: Demand the role places on working respectfully across cultures and backgrounds, fostering inclusion, and ensuring all perspectives are heard. NACE notes this competency is currently under review.

levels.1: Engagement with diversity and inclusion is incidental rather than designed into the role. The postholder works mainly within a small, homogeneous team and only occasionally encounters people from different backgrounds, with no particular expectation to adapt how they work. Basic everyday courtesy is enough, and no specific cultural awareness, inclusive practice, or work with varied communities is asked for. The job neither serves diverse populations directly nor requires navigating different norms or perspectives. Sensitivity to difference might come up by chance but is never a stated requirement of the work.
levels.2: The role involves working respectfully within a diverse team or workplace. Basic cultural sensitivity, inclusive everyday conduct, and the ability to collaborate comfortably with colleagues from a range of backgrounds are expected. The substance of the work, though, is not built around different communities, perspectives, or equity goals. The postholder is asked to be a considerate teammate rather than to demonstrate deep cultural competency or to design for diverse audiences. Inclusion shows up as good behavior within the team rather than as a core function of the job.
levels.3: Working inclusively across difference is a meaningful and recurring part of the role. The postholder regularly works with people from varied backgrounds, adapts to different norms and communication styles, and is actively expected to help ensure everyone is heard and included. A real portion of the work involves serving or collaborating with diverse users, clients, or colleagues, so being attuned to difference affects how well the job is done. The role might involve supporting varied populations, working across regions or groups, or factoring different needs into decisions. Sensitivity to equity and inclusion is part of doing the role well, not just a nicety.
levels.4: The role is substantially about engaging fairly and effectively across cultures and communities. The postholder works with diverse colleagues, clients, or beneficiaries, navigates differing values and expectations, and is responsible for inclusive and equitable practice within their area. Much of the work depends on building trust across difference, tailoring approaches to varied groups, and representing the organization fairly to a range of stakeholders. They may lead inclusion efforts, manage relationships across cultures, or design programs and services for varied populations. Strong cultural competency and an equity-minded outlook are core to performing the role.
levels.5: Equity and inclusion sit at the heart of the role. The work centers on serving diverse or marginalized communities, championing inclusion and fairness, building deep cross-cultural relationships, and making decisions where cultural understanding and equitable impact are decisive. The postholder is expected to lead across difference, mediate competing values and perspectives, and hold responsibility for equitable outcomes. The role might involve diversity and inclusion leadership, community advocacy, or mission-driven work where fairness and belonging are central. Without a genuine, sustained commitment to equity and the skill to work inclusively across many backgrounds, the role could not be carried out.

leadership.definition: Demand the role places on guiding, motivating, and influencing others toward goals, with or without a formal title.

levels.1: Leadership is not really part of the role. The postholder is responsible for their own tasks and works under direction, with no expectation to guide, motivate, or coordinate anyone else. Decisions about how the work is organized are made by others, and the person is asked to contribute rather than to take charge. Initiative beyond their own duties is neither required nor especially looked for. The role suits someone content to do good work within a structure set by others.
levels.2: Occasional initiative and informal influence are welcome but not core. The postholder may take the lead on a small task, help orient a new colleague, or speak up with suggestions, but they are not responsible for guiding others or driving group decisions. Most direction still comes from above, and leadership shows up as being a proactive, dependable team member rather than someone steering the work. The role rewards a willing, self-starting attitude without asking the person to own outcomes for a group. Influence is light and situational.
levels.3: Taking initiative and informally guiding others is an expected part of the role. The postholder is trusted to lead on tasks or small efforts, coordinate the work of peers, help the group reach decisions, and motivate those around them without necessarily holding a title. They are expected to step up, take ownership of pieces of work, and help keep things moving. The role values someone who can organize others and drive progress, even within a flat structure. Lightweight leadership is part of doing the job well.
levels.4: Leadership is a major part of the role. The postholder leads projects or initiatives, motivates and influences others, drives decisions, and is expected to take real ownership of outcomes that depend on a group. They may mentor or coordinate colleagues, set direction for a piece of work, and represent their team's efforts to others. The role rewards the ability to bring people along, resolve disagreements, and deliver results through others as much as through their own work. This is the kind of role described as leading, owning, or driving an area, often with informal or emerging authority.
levels.5: Leadership is the core of the role and carries real accountability. The postholder manages or develops a team, sets direction and strategy, influences across the organization, and is answerable for the performance and outcomes of others. They are expected to motivate at scale, make and own consequential decisions, navigate competing interests, and represent their area to senior stakeholders. The role might involve formal people management, leading a function, or driving change that depends on aligning many others. Strong leadership is essential, and the role could not be performed by someone unable to guide, influence, and take responsibility for a group.

professionalism.definition: Demand the role places on reliability, accountability, ethical conduct, and maturity in the workplace.

levels.1: Basic professional conduct is enough. The postholder is asked mainly to be reliable, turn up on time, and follow simple workplace expectations, with close supervision and a forgiving environment for early mistakes. Little prior experience, polish, or independent judgment about professional matters is required. The role provides structure and oversight, so well-developed workplace habits are helpful but not necessary. It suits people new to work or to the field, where professionalism is still being built.
levels.2: A baseline of workplace readiness is expected. The postholder should show dependable conduct, punctuality, follow-through, and the ability to work appropriately within a team, with some prior exposure to a work environment helpful but not essential. The role assumes the person can carry themselves suitably day to day without yet having a deep professional track record. Sound everyday habits and a responsible attitude matter more than polish or autonomy. Trust is given for routine matters, with bigger calls still overseen.
levels.3: Solid, consistent professionalism is required. The postholder is expected to bring established workplace habits, dependable and ethical conduct, and the ability to manage their responsibilities with limited supervision. The role assumes self-management, good judgment about professional norms, and the maturity to handle commitments without close oversight. Meeting deadlines, owning mistakes, and behaving appropriately with colleagues and others are taken as given. The person should already be comfortable operating in a professional setting.
levels.4: Strong professional readiness is central to the role. It demands proven dependability, mature and ethical conduct, a credible workplace presence, and the ability to handle responsibility and sometimes sensitive or confidential matters with sound judgment. The postholder is expected to operate with autonomy, represent the organization appropriately, and inspire confidence in colleagues and stakeholders. Consistent professionalism under pressure and the ability to navigate workplace situations well are real assets. The role suits an established professional rather than someone still finding their footing.
levels.5: A highly developed professional standard is essential. The role requires consistent, polished professionalism, strong ethical judgment, and the credibility and presence to operate in high-stakes or sensitive settings from day one. The postholder may handle confidential, fiduciary, or reputationally important matters, represent the organization with authority, and be trusted to act on its behalf where lapses would carry serious consequences. Deep reliability, integrity, and the ability to navigate senior or sensitive environments are core to the role. Someone without a substantial, proven professional foundation could not credibly hold it.

teamwork.definition: Demand the role places on collaborating with others, sharing work fairly, resolving conflict, and supporting shared goals.

levels.1: Teamwork is incidental. The postholder works largely on their own tasks, with only occasional, simple contact with others to hand off work or confirm details. Collaboration is not built into the role, and success depends mostly on individual effort rather than on working closely with a group. There is little need to coordinate, share responsibility, or navigate group dynamics. The role suits someone who can work independently within a structure where others' work is largely separate from their own.
levels.2: Routine collaboration within a team is a normal part of the work. The postholder works alongside colleagues, shares information, contributes their part to shared tasks, and gets along within a group. Cooperation is expected at an everyday level, but the work does not heavily depend on tight coordination, managing conflict, or close interdependence with others. Being a reliable, agreeable team member is enough. The role rewards someone who fits in and does their share without friction.
levels.3: Working effectively as part of a team is a real and expected demand. The postholder collaborates closely with colleagues toward shared goals, divides and shares work, supports teammates, and contributes to group outcomes that depend on people pulling together. They are expected to coordinate, communicate within the group, and help smooth over everyday disagreements. The role values someone who strengthens the team and helps it deliver, not just someone who does their own part. Genuine cooperation is part of doing the job well.
levels.4: Teamwork is a major part of the role and often cross-functional. The postholder collaborates across teams or disciplines, coordinates with people who have different priorities, takes shared ownership of deliverables, and helps resolve conflict constructively. Much of the work depends on building good working relationships, aligning others, and making collaboration succeed where interests do not automatically agree. They may bridge between groups or hold a team's joint output together. The role suits someone who makes interdependent, multi-party work run smoothly.
levels.5: The role is fundamentally collaborative and depends on it to succeed. The postholder works in a tightly interdependent setting where outcomes are produced jointly, cross-functional partnership is the core mode of work, and success rests on building consensus, aligning diverse parties, and resolving conflict among people with competing interests. They are expected to hold complex collaborations together, navigate group dynamics under pressure, and share accountability for collective results. The role might involve coordinating across many stakeholders or leading work that no individual could deliver alone. Without strong collaboration skills, the role could not be performed.

technology.definition: Demand the role places on using tools, software, and systems effectively to do the work (Excel, Python, GitHub, Figma, databases, AI tools, or role-specific software).

levels.1: Technology use is minimal and basic. The postholder needs only everyday computer skills, such as email, simple documents, or a basic in-house system, and most of the work is done through familiar, low-complexity tools or by hand. No specialized software, technical skill, or troubleshooting is expected, and support is available when something goes wrong. Comfort with basic tools helps, but the role is not built around technology. It suits work where tools are incidental to the task.
levels.2: Routine use of common tools is part of the job. The postholder works regularly with standard software such as spreadsheets, office applications, or a workplace platform, and is expected to be reasonably comfortable picking up the systems the role uses day to day. The tools are common and learnable rather than specialized, and the work does not depend on deep technical skill or on building anything technical. Being competent and unflustered with everyday software is enough. The role rewards general digital fluency rather than expertise.
levels.3: Solid proficiency with role-relevant tools is expected. The postholder uses specific software or systems central to the work, such as advanced spreadsheets, a CRM, an analytics or design tool, or a role-specific platform, and is comfortable learning new systems as needed. Technical skill is a real and recurring part of doing the job, though the role is defined by its function rather than by the technology itself. They are expected to work capably in the relevant tools without constant support. Reasonable technical confidence is part of doing the job well.
levels.4: Technical skill is a major part of the role. The postholder works daily in specialized tools and systems, such as programming or scripting languages, data platforms, design software, or developer tooling like Python, SQL, GitHub, or Figma, and a meaningful share of their value comes from technical capability. They are expected to use these tools to build, analyze, or create rather than just operate them, and to handle technical complexity with limited hand-holding. The role assumes genuine hands-on expertise in the relevant technologies. This is the kind of role described as technical, data-driven, or tool-intensive.
levels.5: The role is fundamentally technical and built around deep mastery of tools and systems. The postholder is expected to design, build, or engineer with advanced technologies, command relevant programming languages, frameworks, or platforms at an expert level, and solve hard technical problems as the central purpose of the job. The work depends on sustained, high-level technical skill, often at the cutting edge of the relevant tools, where the quality of what they build directly determines results. The role might involve software or data engineering, technical architecture, or specialist technical creation. Without deep, hands-on technical expertise, the role could not be performed.
